summary: This is a brief news post with two unrelated items. Joel announces a community dinner meetup in Rome for August 2004, continuing a tradition of reader gatherings in various cities. He then shares a quote from RFC 3117 about how Postel's robustness principle paradoxically causes deployment problems, since liberal acceptance of errors masks bugs in new implementations until they encounter stricter systems.
key_points: Joel organized informal community dinners with readers in cities around the world, building an in-person community around his blog
Postel's robustness principle ('be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept') can backfire by hiding implementation errors during initial deployment
Tolerant systems create a false sense of correctness that only breaks down when less-liberal implementations are encountered later
money_quotes: Counter-intuitively, Postel's robustness principle ('be conservative in what you send, liberal in what you accept') often leads to deployment problems.

When a new implementation is initially fielded, it is likely that it will encounter only a subset of existing implementations. If those implementations follow the robustness principle, then errors in the new implementation will likely go undetected.
The reader should be able to figure out what happens next.
key_insight: Being too liberal in accepting malformed input paradoxically causes worse interoperability problems down the line by masking bugs during initial deployment.
